request.question: Which Liberian politician was the granddaugher of a market woman?

title: Joseph Korto
Alex J. Tyler
Jackson Doe
Roland Massaquoi
Sirleaf Market Women's Fund
Ruth Perry
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf
Nathaniel Barnes
Charles Brumskine
Samuel George Harmon
sentences: Joseph Korto is a Liberian politician and member of the Liberia Equal Rights Party (LERP).
Joseph Korto was born in Barpa, Nimba County, Liberia.
He was Minister of Education in Liberia from 2006-2010 and was replaced by E. Othello Gongar, formal Minister of Education during the regime of late president Samuel Kanyon Doe.
He is also the Executive Director of the Liberian Development Foundation.
Alex Jenekai Tyler (born 15 December 1963) is a Liberian politician who has been Speaker of the House of Representatives of Liberia since 2007.
He was first elected as the Representative for the 1st District of Bomi County in 2005.
Tyler is a member of the Unity Party, having joined in 2009 following its merger with his Liberian Action Party.
Jackson Doe was a Liberian politician in the late twentieth century and a native of Nimba County.

Immediately before the 1980 military coup d'état, Doe served as Nimba County's senior senator, but when the government was overthrown, he lost his office, was tried for treason, and imprisoned at the Barclay Training Center.
Rebel leader Samuel Doe (no relation) released him from prison at the end of June 1980 but forbade him to leave the country.
In the country's 1985 elections, he ran for president as the candidate of the Liberian Action Party against Samuel Doe, who had been the country's head of state since the coup.
According to official results, Samuel received a tiny majority of votes in the election.

However, many foreign observers alleged fraud and suggested that Jackson was the true victor; according to organizations such as the BBC, Jackson had won an absolute majority of votes cast nationwide.
Jackson's running mate was Emmanuel S. Kroma.
Roland Massaquoi is a Liberian politician and, as of 2006, leader of the National Patriotic Party (NPP).
In 2009, Massaquoi was the President of the Liberian Produce Marketing Corporation.
During 14 years of savage civil war, Liberia’s market women kept food and supplies flowing against terrible odds.
As the conflict worsened, they gathered together praying for peace, then protesting and finally politicking in the international arena.

After peace took hold, in 2005 they turned their hard-won organizational skills to get out the vote for Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, Africa’s first woman to be elected president—the granddaughter of a market woman.
Ruth Sando Fahnbulleh Perry (July 16, 1939 – January 8, 2017) was a Liberian politician.
She served as the interim Chairwoman of the Council of State of Liberia from 3 September 1996 until 2 August 1997, following the First Liberian Civil War.
After eleven international peace attempts between 1990 and 1995 to end the civil war in Liberia, the attempts appeared to succeed.

The interim Council of State consisted of a civilian chair, as well as members of warring factions: Charles Taylor, United Liberation Movement of Liberia for Democracy-K leader Alhaji Kromah, Liberia Peace Council leader George Boley, and two other civilians.
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf (born 29 October 1938) is a Liberian politician who serves as the 24th and current President of Liberia since 2006.
Sirleaf is the first elected female head of state in Africa.
Milton Nathaniel Barnes (born 6 April 1954) is a Liberian politician and member of the Liberian Destiny Party (LDP).
Charles Walker Brumskine (born 27 April 1951) is a Liberian politician and attorney.
He is the Political Leader of the Liberty Party and came third in the 2005 presidential election.
He is considered the most popular opposition politician in Liberia, and he challenged Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf for the Presidency in 2011.
He is also the senior partner of Brumskine & Associates, a leading Liberian law firm.
Samuel George Harmon (born 1861 died 1925) was a Liberian politician.
Harmon was the Vice President of Liberia from January 1, 1912 to January 5, 1920 under President Daniel Edward Howard.
He was a member of the True Whig Party which dominated Liberian politics from 1878 to 1980.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Which Liberian politician was the granddaugher of a market woman?
hop1_docs: Roland Massaquoi | Roland Massaquoi is a Liberian politician and, as of 2006, leader of the National Patriotic Party (NPP).  In 2009, Massaquoi was the President of the Liberian Produce Marketing Corporation.
List of Liberian politicians | The following is a list of Liberian politicians, both past and present.

Samuel Kofi Woods | Samuel Kofi Woods (born January 5, 1964) is a Liberian human rights activist, journalist, politician and academic.  In 1994, he founded the Forefront Organization, which documented human rights abuses during the Second Liberian Civil War.

Samuel George Harmon | Samuel George Harmon (born 1861 died 1925) was a Liberian politician.  Harmon was the Vice President of Liberia from January 1, 1912 to January 5, 1920 under President Daniel Edward Howard.  He was a member of the True Whig Party which dominated Liberian politics from 1878 to 1980.

Charles D. B. King | Charles Dunbar Burgess King (12 March 1875 – 4 September 1961) was a politician in Liberia of Americo-Liberian and Freetown Creole descent (his mother was an Americo-Liberian).  He was a member of the True Whig Party, which ruled the country from 1878 until 1980.  He served as the 17th President of Liberia from 1920 until 1930.
Robert Kpoto | Robert Kpoto is a Liberian orthopedic surgeon, politician, and member of the Union of Liberian Democrats (ULD).

Charles Taylor (Liberian politician) | Charles McArthur Ghankay Taylor (born 28 January 1948) is a former Liberian politician who was the 22nd President of Liberia, serving from 2 August 1997 until his resignation on 11 August 2003.
response.summary_1:
Facts found in the passages:
- [1] Roland Massaquoi — "leader of the National Patriotic Party (NPP)."  
- [2] List of Liberian politicians — page is a "list of Liberian politicians."  
- [3] Samuel Kofi Woods — described as "Liberian human rights activist, journalist, politician."  
- [4] Samuel George Harmon — "Vice President of Liberia from January 1, 1912 to January 5, 1920."  
- [5] Charles D. B. King — "served as the 17th President of Liberia from 1920 until 1930."  
- [6] Robert Kpoto — "Liberian orthopedic surgeon, politician."  
- [7] Charles Taylor — "former Liberian politician ... 22nd President of Liberia."

Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
- The target bridge entity is "the Liberian politician who was the granddaughter of a market woman." No passage contains the words "granddaughter," "grandchild," "market woman," or equivalent family detail — that fact is missing.
- The query does not name a politician; none of the provided pages match or state the required family detail, so the identity is unresolved.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve the missing fact:
1. Fetch the Wikipedia page "Ellen Johnson Sirleaf" — check "Early life", "Family", or search the page for keywords: "market", "market woman", "granddaughter", "grandmother".
2. Fetch the Wikipedia page "Leymah Gbowee" — check "Early life"/"Personal life" and search for the same keywords.
3. Fetch the Wikipedia page "Jewel Howard-Taylor" (and variant "Jewel Cianeh Taylor") — check "Early life"/"Family" sections and search for the keywords.
4. Fetch other prominent female Liberian politicians' pages (e.g., "Florence Chenoweth", "Ellen Cooper") — check "Early life"/"Family" and search for keywords.
5. Fetch the full "List of Liberian politicians" page (passage [2]) and follow links to individual biographies of female/present-day politicians; for each, extract "Early life"/"Family" or search for the keywords "market", "market woman", "granddaughter".
6. If Wikipedia pages above do not show the fact, fetch news articles or authoritative biographies (e.g., BBC, New York Times profiles) for the candidate politicians and search those for "granddaughter of a market woman" or equivalent phrasing.

No final answer is given because the required family fact is not present or unambiguous in the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf granddaughter of a market woman early life
hop2_docs: Sirleaf Market Women's Fund | During 14 years of savage civil war, Liberia’s market women kept food and supplies flowing against terrible odds.  As the conflict worsened, they gathered together praying for peace, then protesting and finally politicking in the international arena.  After peace took hold, in 2005 they turned their hard-won organizational skills to get out the vote for Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, Africa’s first woman to be elected president—the granddaughter of a market woman.

Ellen Johnson Sirleaf | Ellen Johnson Sirleaf (born 29 October 1938) is a Liberian politician who serves as the 24th and current President of Liberia since 2006.  Sirleaf is the first elected female head of state in Africa.

Olubanke King Akerele | Olubanke King Akerele (born May 11, 1946) is a Liberian politician and diplomat who served as the Minister of Foreign Affairs in the cabinet of Ellen Johnson Sirleaf from October 2007 until her resignation on 3 November 2010.  She is the granddaughter of Liberia's 17th president, Charles D. B. King.

Iron Ladies of Liberia | The Iron Ladies of Liberia (2007) is an independently produced documentary film that gives behind-the-scenes access to President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf's first year in government.  Johnson-Sirleaf is Africa's first female president.

Leymah Gbowee | Leymah Roberta Gbowee (born 1 February 1972) is a Liberian peace activist responsible for leading a women's peace movement, Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace that helped bring an end to the Second Liberian Civil War in 2003.  Her efforts to end the war, along with her collaborator Ellen Johnson Sirleaf, helped usher in a period of peace and enabled a free election in 2005 that Sirleaf won.  She, along with Ellen Johnson Sirleaf and Tawakkul Karman, were awarded the 2011 Nobel Peace Prize "for their non-violent struggle for the safety of women and for women's rights to full participation in peace-building work."

George Wallace (diplomat) | George W. Wallace Jr. (born on May 30, 1938) was the foreign minister of Liberia from 2006 to 2007.  He took office early in 2006, having been appointed to the cabinet of the incoming president Ellen Johnson Sirleaf and confirmed by the Senate.  Wallace has had a long career as a Liberian diplomat and ambassador through all of its governments during the past several decades.  In a cabinet reshuffle on August 22, 2007, he was replaced as foreign minister by Olubanka King Akerele and became special adviser to President Johnson Sirleaf instead.

Augustine Kpehe Ngafuan | Augustine Kpehe Ngafuan (born April 7, 1970) served from 2012 to 2015 as the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Liberia, in the administration of President Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf.  Ngafuan was Minister of Finance for Liberia during Johnson-Sirleaf's first term, and was then appointed to his post at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on January 17, 2012.  He took office on February 10, 2012, succeeding Toga McIntosh.
response.summary_2: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf is the Liberian politician who was the granddaughter of a market woman. Ellen Johnson Sirleaf is a Liberian politician who has served as the 24th President of Liberia since 2006 and is Africa's first elected female head of state.
